summary: This is a brief teaser post promoting Joel Spolsky's Inc. magazine article titled 'Good System, Bad System.' The post opens with a vivid, humorous observation about the density of Starbucks locations on his walk to work in midtown Manhattan, using it as a hook to draw readers to the full article. It also promotes his second book, 'More Joel on Software.' The actual substance of the argument about good vs. bad systems is in the linked Inc. article, not in this post itself.
key_points: The post serves primarily as a teaser for an external Inc. magazine article about systems
Spolsky uses the absurd density of Starbucks in Manhattan as a comedic opening hook
He observes how New Yorkers micro-optimize their walking routes out of habit, never exploring alternatives
The post also promotes his second book 'More Joel on Software'
money_quotes: I pass six Starbucks every morning on my walk to work.
For most New Yorkers, micro-optimizing the walk to work is a matter of habit, and I have no reason to cross the street.
For all I know, the other side of Eighth Avenue consists of nothing but pachinko parlors and flea circuses. Wouldn't surprise me one bit.
key_insight: This is a promotional teaser post whose brief excerpt illustrates Spolsky's signature style of using witty everyday observations as entry points into broader business and systems thinking.
